standard_description: Understand division as an unknown-factor problem. For example, find 32 ÷ 8 by finding the number that makes 32 when multiplied by 8.
sources: NCTM Progressions for the Common Core State Standards in Mathematics — Operations and Algebraic Thinking K-5, 2011
EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Teacher Edition — Topic E: Multiplication and Division Using Units of 2 and 3, 2013
Fuson, K. C., & Beckmann, S. (2012). Standard algorithms in the Common Core State Standards. NCSM Journal of Mathematics Education Leadership, 14(2), 14-30.
Carpenter, T. P., Fennema, E., Franke, M. L., Levi, L., & Empson, S. B. (2014). Children's Mathematics: Cognitively Guided Instruction (2nd ed.). Heinemann.
Common Core State Standards Initiative (2010). CCSS for Mathematics — Grade 3.

misconceptions.short_label: Treats a÷b and b÷a as the same problem
misconceptions.description: Student does not recognize that division is non-commutative; reads 32÷8 and 8÷32 as equivalent and produces the same answer for both. Often arises when students rely on the visual shape of the expression rather than the meaning of dividend and divisor.
misconceptions.example: Asked '32 ÷ 8 = ?', student writes 8 ÷ 32 = 4. Or reverses depending on which number 'looks easier'.
misconceptions.citation: EngageNY G3 Module 1 Lesson 17 Teacher Edition — 'Students often invert dividend and divisor when the divisor is the larger-looking number; explicit unit-language (groups-of) is required.'

misconceptions.short_label: Cannot use known multiplication facts to solve division
misconceptions.description: Student treats division as a wholly separate operation and does not search for the unknown factor in a related multiplication fact. They may attempt repeated subtraction unreliably or guess.
misconceptions.example: Asked '32 ÷ 8 = ?', student does not think '8 × ? = 32' even though they know 8 × 4 = 32. Instead they subtract 8 repeatedly and lose count.
misconceptions.citation: NCTM Progressions OA K-5 (2011), p.27 — 'Division is profitably treated as finding an unknown factor; students who do not make this connection rely on inefficient strategies.'

misconceptions.short_label: Subtracts divisor once and reports difference
misconceptions.description: Student treats ÷ as a single subtraction, producing dividend − divisor as the answer. This indicates the division symbol has not been distinguished from minus.
misconceptions.citation: Carpenter et al. (2014) Children's Mathematics — Symbol-level confusions between ÷ and − are common in early G3 before measurement and partitive models are stabilized.

misconceptions.short_label: Forces the smaller number to be the divisor
misconceptions.description: Student believes division must always have the smaller number as divisor regardless of context, producing the wrong quotient when the problem text places the larger number as divisor.
misconceptions.example: Word problem: 'A box holds 4 cookies. We have 24 cookies. How many boxes?' Student computes 4 ÷ 24, gets 0 or refuses.
misconceptions.citation: Behr, M.J., Lesh, R., Post, T.R. (1983) — 'Order-irrelevance assumptions transfer from addition; explicit instruction on partitive vs. measurement is required.'

misconceptions.short_label: Skip-counts to find factor but loses count or skips wrong number
misconceptions.description: Student attempts to find the unknown factor by skip-counting by the divisor but miscounts (off-by-one) or skip-counts by the wrong number (off the unit).
misconceptions.example: 32 ÷ 8: counts '8, 16, 24, 32' but loses track of how many counts (says 3 or 5 instead of 4). Or skip-counts by 4 instead of 8.
misconceptions.citation: EngageNY G3 Module 1 Lesson 18 — 'Skip-counting is a valid bridge strategy but introduces tally errors; require students to record counts.'

misconceptions.short_label: Drops the remainder or adds it to the quotient
misconceptions.description: When a problem does not divide evenly, student either silently drops the remainder or adds it onto the quotient. At G3 most problems are exact, but in word problems with leftovers students still mishandle the leftover.
misconceptions.example: 20 ÷ 3 → student writes 7 (rounding up) or 6 (silently drops the remainder of 2).
misconceptions.citation: NCTM Progressions OA K-5 (2011), p.28 — Treatment of remainders is context-dependent; students must learn to interpret the situation.

misconceptions.short_label: Confuses 'number of groups' with 'objects per group' in the quotient
misconceptions.description: Student computes the right number but assigns it to the wrong role in the word problem (says 4 cookies in each box instead of 4 boxes).
misconceptions.example: '24 cookies, 6 per box, how many boxes?' Student writes 24 ÷ 6 = 4 but answers '4 cookies'. Or reverses the question entirely.
misconceptions.citation: Carpenter et al. (2014) — Partitive (sharing) vs. measurement (grouping) distinction is foundational; G3 students benefit from unit-language drills.

misconceptions.short_label: Applies doubling/halving without checking with multiplication
misconceptions.description: Student uses a half-remembered rule (e.g., 'divide means cut in half') and halves the dividend regardless of divisor.
misconceptions.citation: EngageNY G3 Module 1 — 'Students apply half-remembered procedures from earlier grades; require verification with multiplication.'
